questions.prompt: What is the near-term driver of Starship demand and economics?

choices.text: Starlink v2. Starlink v2 satellites are roughly 5x heavier than v1 and won't fit on Falcon 9 — they need Starship. SpaceX wants tens of thousands of v2 satellites in orbit, which requires dozens of Starship launches per year. This internal demand from a SpaceX-owned customer (Starlink) sustains the launch cadence that makes the rest of Starship economics work. Lunar Artemis HLS missions arrive next (multi-billion-dollar NASA contracts). Mars settlement is multi-decade long-term. Earth-to-Earth is speculative. The near-term economics are Starlink-driven; everything else builds on that base of demonstrated operational reliability and high cadence.

questions.explanation: Starlink v2 is the near-term economic engine. It justifies Starship's existence in revenue terms while the longer-term missions develop. Without Starlink-scale internal demand, Starship's launch cadence and unit economics would be much harder to achieve.

questions.prompt: Why is in-orbit propellant transfer the critical technology unlock for Starship's lunar and Mars missions?

choices.text: Without refueling, Starship can deliver ~100 tons to LEO but progressively less to higher destinations because most of the delta-v from LEO to (say) lunar transfer must come from propellant carried up from Earth — eating into payload. With orbital refueling, a mission Starship reaches LEO with its payload, receives propellant from 6-15 tanker Starships in LEO, then departs for the destination with a full propellant load. This effectively converts LEO launch capacity into deep-space delivery capacity. Without it: Starship is a heavy LEO launcher with marginal beyond-LEO capability. With it: Starship can do lunar and Mars missions at scale. This is why SpaceX is testing intra-vehicle propellant transfer now and inter-vehicle transfer next, and why high launch cadence isn't optional — refueling requires multiple tanker launches per mission.

questions.explanation: Refueling is the architectural keystone. Lunar and Mars missions don't work without it; with it, they become technically feasible. The cadence requirement that follows (multiple tanker launches per mission) is why Starship's manufacturing scale and launch rate matter so much.
